studyMetadata.subtitle1: Who it’s for, what it’s all about, and whether it’s for you
studyMetadata.description1: This study is in four main sections. Each of the first three sections has a passage from the Bible which talks about baptism. Alongside it are some questions about that passage, and some other verses from the Bible to help you understand what’s going on. Section four gives space to plan the more practical details of your baptism.
studyMetadata.author: Jay Parsons
studyMetadata.shareMessage:
Check out this Bible study app — “Grow in Christ”.

Available soon on Google Play and the App Store.

theme.theme-annotation: Terracotta study theme — warm ochre, burnt sienna, and hillside green palette. Grounded and vibrant, evoking African earth tones, harvest, and communal labour. Suited to studies on identity, calling, womanhood, or vocation. Warm and energising rather than restful.

elements.question1: These are the four main passages we will work through together across this study. It’s worth bookmarking them now.

elements.text1:
Ruth had been a Christian for three months. She had given her life to Jesus at an evening service, quietly, with tears running down her face, while the congregation sang. Her pastor had prayed with her. Her friend had hugged her for a long time afterwards.

In the weeks that followed, something had genuinely changed. She read her Bible in the early morning before the house woke up. She prayed — not perfectly, but honestly. She had told her sister about Jesus, which had taken more courage than she expected.

But every Sunday, when the church announced an upcoming baptism, she felt something she could not quite name. A pull. A question. A sense that there was something she had not yet done, something that was waiting for her.

She had been baptised as an infant, in a white dress, with her parents and grandparents gathered around. There were photographs. Her mother spoke of it warmly.

Was that enough? she wondered. Or is this something I need to do for myself?

This study is for people like Ruth — people who are thinking seriously about baptism and want to understand what the Bible actually says about it.
What This Study Is For

This is not a study about church tradition, or about what your denomination has always done, or about what your parents expect. It is a study about what the Bible teaches.

By the end of it, you should have a clear understanding of three things:

  • Who baptism is for
  • What baptism is all about — what it means and what it declares
  • Whether it is something you should do, and if so, how to take the next steps

The study is designed to be worked through with a church leader. You may read each chapter on your own and then discuss it together, or you may go through it side by side. Either way, you should not work through it in isolation — baptism is a public act, connected to a local church family, and it is right that a church leader is part of the conversation.

If you are a young person considering baptism, it is important that your parents are involved in thinking it through. Your church leader will know how to help with that.
How to Use This Study

The study has five main chapters, plus this introduction:

  • Chapter 1 — Who is Baptism for?
  • Chapter 2 — What is Baptism all about?
  • Chapter 3 — Baptism and Belonging
  • Chapter 4 — What’s next? (practical preparation)
  • Chapter 5 — My Baptism Notes and Common Questions

Each of the first three chapters contains a Bible passage, some explanation, and questions to think through and discuss. Don’t worry if you find some questions difficult — the point is honest engagement, not correct answers. Some things you will not know yet. That is fine. Your church leader is there to help you.

At the end of each chapter there is a short personal reflection section and a practical challenge for the days ahead.

elements.question1: Before you begin, take a moment to write down what you already think baptism is. Where has that idea come from — your upbringing, your church, something you’ve read or heard?
What is the main question you are hoping this study will answer? What would it mean for you to finish it with clarity?

deeper.question1: Is there anything that makes you hesitant or nervous about the idea of being baptised — a person whose reaction you are worried about, a question you are afraid to ask, or a doubt you have not voiced yet? It is worth naming it honestly at the start.

elements.question1: What if you are not sure whether you are a Christian yet — can you still work through this study?
elements.answer1:
Yes, absolutely. This study will help you think through not only baptism but also what it means to become a Christian in the first place. Chapter 1 deals directly with that question. You do not need to have everything settled before you begin — that is precisely what this study is for.

If, as you work through it, you find that you are not yet sure of your faith in Jesus Christ, your church leader can help you think that through too. Coming to faith and being baptised are closely connected in the New Testament, and your church leader will be glad to walk with you through both.

elements.term1: “I was already baptised as a baby.”
elements.question1: If you were baptised as an infant, does this study still apply to you?
elements.answer1:
This is one of the most common questions people bring to a study like this, and it is a good one. Different Christian traditions have different views on infant baptism, and this study will address that question honestly in Chapter 1 and in the bonus questions at the end.

The short answer for now is: yes, this study still applies to you. It will help you understand what baptism means, what the Bible teaches about who it is for, and — with your church leader — how to think through your own situation carefully.

Ruth, in our opening story, faced exactly this question. She is not alone.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement. There are no right or wrong answers — this is simply a starting point for honest reflection.
statements1: I have a clear understanding of what baptism means and why Christians do it.
I feel confident that I have personally put my faith in Jesus Christ.
I feel ready to discuss baptism openly with a church leader.
I understand the difference between being baptised as an infant and being baptised as a believer.
I am approaching this study with a genuine desire to do whatever the Bible teaches, even if it is not what I expected.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Ruth felt a pull she could not quite name — a sense that something was waiting for her. Is there anything in her story that resonates with where you are right now?
As you begin this study, what is your honest prayer? What are you asking God to show you or settle in you by the end of it?

elements.text1:
Baptism is one of the most significant public acts a Christian can take. It is a declaration to the watching world — to your family, your community, and your church — of what Jesus has done for you and in you.

It is not something to be rushed into without understanding. But it is also not something to be put off indefinitely out of uncertainty or fear. Jesus commanded it. His first followers took it seriously. And Christians have been doing it, all over the world and across the centuries, ever since.

This study exists to help you understand it well — so that if and when you are baptised, you know exactly what you are doing, and why.

Let’s begin.

elements.question1: What should you do before the next chapter?
elements.answer1:
Read Acts 2:36–41 — the passage at the heart of Chapter 1. Read it slowly, more than once if you can. Notice what it says, what surprises you, and what questions it raises.

Then write down one question you want to bring to the discussion.

elements.question1: Read this passage carefully before you begin — and keep it open as you work through the chapter.

elements.text1:
John had grown up in the church. He had been in Sunday school since he could walk, had memorised more Bible verses than most of the adults around him, and had never — as far as anyone could tell — gone through a phase of rebellion or doubt. He was, by every measure, a good young man from a good Christian family.

When he was seventeen, something shifted. During a week of prayer at his church, he found himself reading Romans 8 alone in his room late at night, and for the first time something moved from his head to somewhere deeper. He was not just reading about Jesus — he was, he realised, trusting him. Depending on him. Not because his parents did, not because it was what his church expected, but because he believed it was true and it was his only hope.

He went to his pastor the following week and said he wanted to be baptised.

His pastor asked him a question that stopped him short: “John — are you a Christian?”

Not “are you from a Christian family?” Not “have you been in church all your life?” But: are you, personally, a Christian?

That is the question this chapter begins with. Because before we can ask who baptism is for, we need to ask what it means to be a Christian — and whether that description fits you.
Part 1 — The First Christian Sermon

To understand who baptism is for, we need to go back to the very beginning of the church — to the day when the first group of people were ever baptised as Christians.

Jesus had died on the cross as a perfect sacrifice, taking the punishment for the sins of his people. He had been buried, and then — because God was fully satisfied with his sacrifice — he had risen from death three days later. He had returned to heaven to reign as King alongside God the Father. And then, just as he had promised, he had sent his Holy Spirit to fill his disciples and enable them to carry his message to the world.

We join the story at a decisive moment. One of Jesus’ disciples, Peter, is preaching the very first Christian sermon to a large crowd in Jerusalem. We pick up at his conclusion.

elements.passageText1:
36“Therefore let all Israel be assured of this: God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Messiah.”

37When the people heard this, they were cut to the heart and said to Peter and the other apostles, “Brothers, what shall we do?”

38Peter replied, “Repent and be baptised, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins. And you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit. 39The promise is for you and your children and for all who are far off — for all whom the Lord our God will call.”

40With many other words he warned them; and he pleaded with them, “Save yourselves from this corrupt generation.” 41Those who accepted his message were baptised, and about three thousand were added to their number that day.
elements.passageText2:
36“Therefore let all the house of Israel know beyond a doubt that God has made this Jesus whom you crucified both Lord and Christ.”

37Now when they heard this, they were acutely distressed and said to Peter and the rest of the apostles, “What should we do, brothers?”

38Peter said to them, “Repent, and each one of you be baptised in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit. 39For the promise is for you and your children, and for all who are far away, as many as the Lord our God will call to himself.”

40With many other words he testified and exhorted them saying, “Save yourselves from this perverse generation!” 41So those who accepted his message were baptised, and that day about three thousand people were added.

elements.question1: Peter tells the crowd that God has made Jesus — the one they crucified — both Lord and Messiah. The people are described as being “cut to the heart.” What do you think that means? What is it about Peter’s message that produces that reaction?

deeper.question1: Have you ever had a moment when something you heard or read about Jesus landed differently — not just as information, but as something that personally confronted or moved you? What was that like?

elements.question1: The crowd asks: “What shall we do?” Peter gives them two instructions in verse 38. What are they — and what do you think each one means?
What does it actually mean to repent? Is it just feeling sorry?
elements.answer1:
Repentance is more than feeling sorry. The word in the original Greek means to change your mind — and that change of mind leads to a change of direction. It means turning away from a life lived on your own terms, and turning toward God and his way.

It is not about achieving a certain level of goodness before you come to God. The people in Acts 2 were baptised on the same day they heard and believed the gospel — they had not yet had time to prove themselves or clean up their lives. What they had done was accept the message, acknowledge their sin, and turn toward Jesus.

Repentance is not a one-time event that you complete perfectly and then move on from. It is the posture of a Christian life — a continual turning back to God when we drift. But it begins with a genuine, personal moment of turning.

elements.question1: Peter says two things are promised to those who repent and are baptised (v38). What are they? What do each of these mean to you personally?
Verse 41 tells us who gets baptised. What is the connection between accepting the message and being baptised — and what does that tell us about the right order of things?

deeper.question1: Is there anything that makes you hesitate to say plainly that you have personally accepted the gospel message — the news that Jesus died for your sins and rose again? If so, what is it?

elements.text1: Part 2 — Am I a Christian?

The passage makes clear that baptism follows believing. So before we go any further, it is worth pausing to ask the most important question in this study — not “should I be baptised?” but “am I a Christian?”

Being a Christian is not the same as growing up in a Christian home, attending church regularly, being a generally good person, or having been baptised or dedicated as a child. All of those things may be true of you. None of them, on their own, make you a Christian.

A Christian is someone who has personally heard the gospel — the news that Jesus Christ died in their place and rose again — and has responded to it with repentance and faith. Not faith inherited from parents. Not faith performed for a congregation. Personal faith. Your own.

elements.question1: In your own words, what is the gospel — the good news about Jesus Christ? Try to explain it as if you were telling someone who had never heard it before.
When did you first hear the gospel? How have you responded to it — not what you have always thought or assumed, but how you have personally, deliberately responded?

deeper.question1: John, in our opening story, described a moment when his faith moved from his head to somewhere deeper — from knowing about Jesus to actually trusting him. Has anything like that happened for you? If you are not sure, what would it mean for that to happen?

elements.term1: “I need to be good enough before I get baptised.”
elements.question1: Should you wait until you have reached a certain level of Christian maturity before being baptised?
elements.answer1:
No. The people baptised in Acts 2 were baptised on the same day they believed — they had not had time to prove themselves, grow in holiness, or demonstrate sustained Christian living. Throughout the New Testament, the pattern is the same: believe, repent, be baptised. There is no waiting period to earn the right.

Baptism is not a reward for mature Christians. It is the first public step of a new Christian. It is for forgiven people, not perfect people — people who have accepted the gospel and turned toward Jesus, and who now want to declare that publicly.

If you are waiting until you feel good enough, you may be waiting a very long time. And you may be misunderstanding what baptism is for.

elements.term1: “I was baptised as a baby — won’t that do?”
elements.question1: Some Christians were baptised as infants. Does that count?
elements.answer1:
This is a genuinely contested question among Christians, and good people disagree about it. Some traditions believe that infant baptism is a valid sign of belonging to a covenant community, anticipating faith that will come later. Other traditions believe that baptism is only meaningful when given to someone who is personally professing faith — and that infant baptism, however well-intentioned, is not what the New Testament has in mind.

The view of this study is that baptism is for people who are able to give a credible, personal profession of faith in Jesus Christ. It is a sign of new spiritual birth — not physical birth into a Christian family.

This does not mean that those who baptise infants are wrong to love and care for their children in that way. But it does mean that if you have come to personal faith in Jesus and have never been baptised as a believer, baptism is something you should prayerfully consider.

Talk this through carefully with your church leader. It is an important conversation, and they will help you think it through well.

elements.text1:
There is one more passage worth reading alongside Acts 2 — a story that shows how seriously the first Christians took the connection between faith and baptism.

In Acts 8:26–40, a man from Ethiopia — a high official in the queen’s court — is reading from the prophet Isaiah when he meets a follower of Jesus named Philip. Philip explains the passage to him, and in doing so tells him the good news about Jesus. As they travel together and pass a body of water, the man asks a simple question: “What is to prevent me from being baptised?”

Nothing, it turns out. He stops the chariot. He goes down into the water. He is baptised. And he goes on his way rejoicing.

He did not wait for a class, or a preparation period, or a slot in the church calendar. The first step of his new life in Jesus was baptism — taken immediately, with joy.

elements.question1: What strikes you about the Ethiopian official’s response to the gospel? What does his urgency tell you about how the first Christians understood baptism?

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I can clearly explain the gospel — the good news about Jesus — in my own words.
I am confident that I have personally repented of my sin and put my faith in Jesus Christ.
I understand that baptism is for people who have personally believed, not just for those from Christian families.
I feel ready to discuss with a church leader whether I am ready to be baptised.
I understand that baptism is not about being good enough — it is about responding to what Jesus has done.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: John’s pastor asked him a simple but searching question: “Are you a Christian?” How would you answer that question — honestly, in your own words, not the answer you think you are supposed to give?
The people in Acts 2 were “cut to the heart” by Peter’s message. Has the gospel ever cut you to the heart in that way? What happened — and what did you do with it?
Based on what you have read in this chapter, do you think baptism is something you should do? What is holding you back — or what is drawing you forward?

elements.text1:
Baptism is not for perfect people. It is not for people who have earned the right through years of faithful service. It is not for people who have been in church the longest, or whose families have the strongest Christian heritage.

It is for people who have heard the gospel, been cut to the heart by it, turned from their sin, and put their trust in the Lord Jesus Christ.

The question is not whether you are good enough. The question is whether you have believed.

elements.question1: What should you do before the next chapter?
elements.answer1:
Read Romans 6:1–14 — the passage at the heart of Chapter 2. As you read it, ask yourself: what does Paul say baptism pictures? What does it say about the old life and the new?

Write down one thing that strikes you — and one question the passage raises for you — to bring to the next discussion.

elements.question1: Read this passage carefully before you begin — and keep it open as you work through the chapter.

elements.text1:
Sarah had watched three baptisms before her own. She had stood at the edge of the pool with the rest of the congregation, singing, while one by one the candidates went down into the water and came back up again. People clapped. Some wept. The pastor said something each time about death and resurrection, about the old life and the new.

She had found it moving. But she was not sure she had understood it.

When it came to her own baptism preparation, she asked her pastor a question she had been carrying for months: “What is actually happening when someone gets baptised? Is it just a symbol? Does the water do something? Am I a Christian before I go in, or does the water make me one?”

Her pastor smiled. He told her those were exactly the right questions. And then he opened his Bible to Romans 6.
Part 1 — Baptism is a Sign

Before we look at the Romans 6 passage, it is important to establish one foundational truth: baptism does not make you a Christian. It is a sign of what has already happened — a visible, public picture of an invisible, inner reality.

Think of a wedding ring. The ring does not create the marriage. The marriage exists because two people made vows and became husband and wife. The ring is a sign — it points to that reality, declares it publicly, and reminds both the wearer and the world of what is true. Removing the ring does not end the marriage. But wearing it matters. It is a meaningful, deliberate declaration.

Baptism works in a similar way. The water does not wash away your sins — Jesus’ death on the cross does that, and it happens the moment you put your faith in him. But baptism is the moment you declare that publicly, visibly, in the presence of your church family and anyone else who is watching. It is the sign of what Jesus has already done in you.

So what does that sign actually depict? The New Testament gives us three interlocking pictures.
Part 2 — A Bath, a Burial, and a Birth

Baptism as a Bath

The Bible is frank about the human condition: we are spiritually unclean. Our sin has made us unfit to stand before a holy God. Left to ourselves, we have no way to deal with that problem.

The good news of the gospel is that Jesus deals with it for us. Through his death on the cross, our sins are washed away. We are made spiritually clean — not because of anything we have done, but because of what he has done. We can now approach God with confidence, accepted and forgiven.

In Acts 22:16, a man called Ananias says to Paul, just after Paul has come to faith in Jesus: “Get up, be baptised and wash your sins away, calling on his name.” The language of washing is the language of baptism — because baptism is a picture of the spiritual cleansing that has already taken place through faith in Christ.

Going under the water and coming back up declares: I was unclean. Now, through Jesus, I am clean.

elements.question1: In what sense are human beings spiritually “unclean” before God? What has Jesus done about that — and how does the image of a bath help explain it?

deeper.question1: Have you experienced a genuine sense of your own sin — not just as a general acknowledgment, but as a personal weight? How did you respond to it, and what difference has Jesus’ forgiveness made to you?

elements.text1:
Baptism as a Burial and a Birth

The second and third pictures come together in the passage we are about to study. They are inseparable.

When you go down into the water, you are enacting a burial. The old life — the life lived in sin, under the power of sin, for yourself rather than for God — is dead and buried. It goes under the water and does not come back.

When you come up out of the water, you are enacting a birth. You are rising into new life — life that is united with the risen Christ, life that is lived for God, life that death no longer has the final word over.

This is not just poetic language. The apostle Paul, in Romans 6, argues that this death and resurrection is spiritually real — that something has genuinely happened to the person who believes in Jesus. Baptism does not cause it. But baptism pictures it with startling clarity.

elements.passageText1:
1What shall we say, then? Shall we go on sinning so that grace may increase? 2By no means! We are those who have died to sin; how can we live in it any longer? 3Or don’t you know that all of us who were baptised into Christ Jesus were baptised into his death? 4We were therefore buried with him through baptism into death in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may live a new life.

5For if we have been united with him in a death like his, we will certainly also be united with him in a resurrection like his. 6For we know that our old self was crucified with him so that the body ruled by sin might be done away with, that we should no longer be slaves to sin — 7because anyone who has died has been set free from sin.

8Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we will also live with him. 9For we know that since Christ was raised from the dead, he cannot die again; death no longer has mastery over him. 10The death he died, he died to sin once for all; but the life he lives, he lives to God.

11In the same way, count yourselves dead to sin but alive to God in Christ Jesus. 12Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its evil desires. 13Do not offer any part of yourself to sin as an instrument of wickedness, but rather offer yourselves to God as those who have been brought from death to life; and offer every part of yourself to him as an instrument of righteousness. 14For sin shall no longer be your master, because you are not under the law, but under grace.
elements.passageText2:
1What then shall we say? Are we to remain in sin so that grace may increase? 2Absolutely not! How can we who died to sin still live in it? 3Or do you not know that as many of us as were baptised into Christ Jesus were baptised into his death? 4Therefore we have been buried with him through baptism into death, in order that just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so we too may live a new life.

5For if we have become united with him in the likeness of his death, we will certainly also be united in the likeness of his resurrection. 6We know that our old man was crucified with him so that the body of sin would no longer dominate us, so that we would no longer be enslaved to sin. 7(For someone who has died has been freed from sin.)

8Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we will also live with him. 9We know that since Christ has been raised from the dead, he is never going to die again; death no longer has mastery over him. 10For the death he died, he died to sin once for all, but the life he lives, he lives to God.

11So you too consider yourselves dead to sin, but alive to God in Christ Jesus. 12Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its desires, 13and do not present your members to sin as instruments to be used for unrighteousness, but present yourselves to God as those who are alive from the dead and your members to God as instruments to be used for righteousness. 14For sin will not be your master, because you are not under law but under grace.

elements.question1: Paul says that being baptised into Christ means being baptised into his death. What do you think he means by that? How is a Christian’s experience connected to what happened to Jesus on the cross?

deeper.question1: Paul uses the phrase “united with him” (v5). What does it mean, in your own experience, to be united with Christ — not just to know about him, but to be joined to him in a living relationship? What difference does that union make day to day?

elements.question1: Paul describes the “old self” as crucified with Christ (v6), so that we are “no longer slaves to sin.” What was your old self like — the version of you that lived without Christ at the centre? In what sense is that self now dead?

Verses 7–10 describe Jesus’ own death and resurrection as the pattern for the Christian life. He died to sin once; he now lives to God. What does it mean for you, practically, to “count yourself dead to sin but alive to God” (v11)?

deeper.question1: Paul does not say that sin becomes impossible for a Christian — he says it should no longer be our master (v14). Is there an area of your life where sin still feels more like a master than a defeated enemy? What does this passage say to you about that?

elements.question1: Look at verses 12–13. Paul moves from theology to instruction — from what is true to what we should therefore do. What does he tell us to offer to God, and what does he tell us to withhold from sin? How is baptism a public declaration of exactly that commitment?
Is baptism just a symbol — or does something real happen when a person is baptised?
elements.answer1:
Baptism is a sign — but that does not mean it is merely a symbol, in the sense of being unimportant or optional. Signs matter. They carry meaning, make declarations, and mark transitions.

What baptism does not do is save you. You are saved by grace through faith in Jesus Christ — full stop. The water cannot add to what his cross has already accomplished.

What baptism does do is several important things: it declares publicly what is already true of you before God; it marks your entry into the life of the local church; it gives you a moment of obedience and commitment that you can look back on; and through the Holy Spirit, it seals upon your heart the reality of what you are declaring. It is a meaningful, significant, commanded act — not a mere formality, but not a second means of salvation either.

Sarah’s question — does the water do something? — is a good one. The answer is: the water declares what Jesus has already done.

elements.term1: “Why go all the way under? Can’t I just be sprinkled?”
elements.question1: Does baptism have to be by full immersion — going completely under the water?
elements.answer1:
The Greek word for “baptise” means to wash, dip, plunge, or submerge — so the weight of the word itself points toward full immersion. And as Romans 6 makes clear, the meaning of baptism is wrapped up in the pictures of burial and resurrection: going down under the water and rising back up. Sprinkling water over someone’s head conveys the picture of washing, but it does not as naturally convey the picture of death, burial, and new birth.

For these reasons, full immersion is the practice commended in this study — it is the method most consistent with the meaning of baptism as the New Testament presents it.

That said, there are situations — such as significant physical disability — where full immersion may not be possible, and a church leader can help think through what is appropriate in those cases.

elements.text1: Part 3 — What Baptism Declares

It is worth pausing to bring together the three pictures and ask: what exactly is a person declaring when they are baptised?

They are declaring, first, that they were spiritually dirty and that Jesus has washed them clean — that their sins are forgiven through his death on the cross.

They are declaring, second, that the old life — the life lived for self, under the power of sin — is over. Dead. Buried. Not coming back.

They are declaring, third, that they have been raised into a new life — a life united with the risen Christ, a life that now belongs to God rather than to sin.

And they are declaring all of this not in a private journal, not in a quiet prayer, but in public — in front of their church, their family, their community. Baptism is an announcement. It says to everyone watching: this is who I am now, this is what Jesus has done for me, and I am not ashamed of it.

elements.question1: Thinking about your own life — your own story of coming to faith — which of the three pictures (bath, burial, birth) feels most significant to you right now, and why?

Baptism is a public declaration. Is there anything about making that declaration publicly — in front of your church, your family, your community — that feels daunting? What might be holding you back from it?

deeper.question1: Romans 6:4 says we are raised “in order that… we too may live a new life.” The point of baptism is not just the moment in the water — it is the life that follows. What does living a new life look like for you, practically, in your home, your relationships, your work?

elements.term1: “Why was Jesus baptised if he had no sin?”
elements.question1: Jesus was baptised by John — but if baptism is about repentance and forgiveness, why did Jesus need it?
elements.answer1:
It is a fair question — and John the Baptist himself raised it (Matthew 3:14). Jesus had not sinned, so he had nothing to repent of. But Jesus told John to go ahead “to fulfil all righteousness.”

There are at least three things going on. First, Jesus was doing what was right — and God the Father confirmed it immediately, declaring from heaven that he was well pleased with his Son. Second, Jesus was giving us a sign of what he was going to do — his own death, burial, and resurrection were the reality to which every believer’s baptism points. Third, Jesus was identifying himself with sinners — standing in the place of the people he came to save, as he would ultimately do on the cross.

Jesus’ baptism was unique. He did not need what baptism declares — forgiveness, new birth, freedom from sin’s power. But by being baptised, he endorsed and inaugurated the practice for all who would follow him.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I understand that baptism is a sign of what Jesus has already done — it does not make me a Christian.
I can explain the three pictures of baptism — bath, burial, and birth — in my own words.
I believe that my old life of sin has genuinely ended and that I am living a new life in Christ.
I understand why full immersion is the method of baptism that best captures what the New Testament teaches.
I feel ready to make the declaration that baptism involves — publicly, in front of my church and community.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Sarah watched three baptisms before her own and found them moving but not fully understood. Has your understanding of what baptism means changed through this chapter? What is clearest to you now that was not before?

Romans 6:4 contains one of the key verses of this study: “We were therefore buried with him through baptism into death in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may live a new life.” Write this verse somewhere you will see it this week. What does it mean for the way you live tomorrow?
If you were baptised tomorrow, what would you be declaring to the people watching? Try to put it in your own words — not theological language, but the language of your own story.

elements.text1:
Baptism is not complicated, but it is profound. It takes a few minutes. It involves water, and a few words, and a church family gathered around you. But in those few minutes, you are enacting — for yourself and for everyone watching — the death of the old life and the birth of the new. You are saying, with your whole body, what the gospel has declared about you: I was lost. I am found. I was dead. I am alive. I was dirty. I am clean. And it is all because of Jesus.

That is worth doing. That is worth doing well. And that is worth understanding before you do it.

elements.question1: What should you do before the next chapter?
elements.answer1:
Read Matthew 28:18–20 and Ephesians 4:1–6 — the two passages at the heart of Chapter 3. As you read them, ask: what does baptism have to do with belonging? What community does it connect you to, and what does that community look like?

Write down one thing that strikes you from each passage, and bring them to the next discussion.

studyOnboardingSlides.body1:
This study will help you understand who baptism is for, what it is all about, and whether it is something you should do.

Work through each section at your own pace alongside a church leader who will help you think it through.
studyOnboardingSlides.body2:
Wannan nazarin zai taimake ka fahimtar wa baftisma yake gare shi, abin da ke cikinta, da kuma ko shi ne abin da ya kamata ka yi.

Karanta kowane ɓangare a kan lokacinka tare da shugaban coci wanda zai taimake ka tunani game da shi.
studyOnboardingSlides.body3:
Janngirde nde ɗon wallitoo ma anndugo ko bomɓo batiisma waɗi, ɗume ɗum fof ɓuri, e so ɗum ko huunde a haani waɗude.

Janngu kooɗume nokku dow sa’a ma tawa bee ganndo laaɓal ngal wallitoo ma miijitugo dow ɗum.

studyOnboardingSlides.body1:
Baptism is for people who have heard the good news of Jesus, repented of their sin, and put their trust in him.

It is not for perfect people — it is for forgiven people who now want to live lives that please God. Acts 2:36–41 shows us that baptism followed straight after believing.
studyOnboardingSlides.body2:
Baftisma yana gare mutanen da suka ji labarin mai dadi na Yesu, suka tuba daga zunubansu, suka kuma dogara gare shi.

Ba don mutane cikakku ba ne — yana don mutanen da aka yafe musu zunubi waɗanda yanzu suke son rayuwa da take faranta wa Allah rai. Ayyukan 2:36–41 yana nuna mana cewa baftisma ya biyo bayan imani nan take.
studyOnboardingSlides.body3:
Batiisma waɗi bomɓo nanii khabaaru moƴƴo Yeesu, toobi dow juuɓe muɓɓen, nden tawii dow makko.

Waɗaa ngam yimɓe fuu timmunɓe — waɗi ngam yimɓe ɓe mpaamaama juuɓe muɓɓen ɓe ŋaaɗii yewtude ngendam nde juutoo Allah. Kuuɗe 2:36–41 hollitiima en batiisma ɗon wari caggal tawagol taccagol.

studyOnboardingSlides.body1: Baptism is a visible sign of three things Jesus has done for you: a bath — your sins washed away; a burial — your old life dead and gone; and a birth — you raised into new life in Christ. Romans 6:1–14 shows that being baptised by full immersion is a picture of dying and rising with Jesus.
studyOnboardingSlides.body2: Baftisma alama ce ta gani ta abubuwa uku da Yesu ya yi dominku: wanka — an wanke zunubanka; jana’iza — rayuwarka ta tsoho ta mutu kuma ta ɓace; da kuma haihuwa — an tashe ka zuwa sabuwar rayuwa a cikin Almasihu. Romawa 6:1–14 yana nuna cewa an nutse gaba ɗaya a cikin ruwa alama ce ta mutuwa da tashin hankali tare da Yesu.
studyOnboardingSlides.body3: Batiisma ko alaamat jeen keewɗe nde yi’etee ngam huunde tati Yeesu waɗannoo ma: lootaari — juuɓe ma lootiima; deeƴre — ngendam ma beli feƴƴiti e yahii; e jibinannde — a tawtinaama ngendam pamɗam nder Almasiihu. Romanko’en 6:1–14 hollitiima batiisma bee njiimuɗam fof nder ndiyam ko seedantaare maatugol maayde e toownaade bee Yeesu.

studyOnboardingSlides.body1:
When you are baptised you are publicly declaring that you now belong to God and to his people, the church.

Jesus commanded his disciples to go and make disciples of all nations, baptising them in the name of the Father, Son and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:18–20). Joining a local church is how you grow, serve, and keep going.
studyOnboardingSlides.body2:
Lokacin da aka yi maka baftisma kana bayyana wa jama’a cewa yanzu kana cikin Allah da mutanensa, cocin.

Yesu ya umarci almajiransa su je su yi almajirai a cikin al’ummai duka, suna yi musu baftisma da suna Uba, Da, da Ruhu Mai Tsarki (Matiyu 28:18–20). Shiga cocin gida-gida shi ne yadda kake girma, yin hidima, da kuma ci gaba.
studyOnboardingSlides.body3:
Wakkati a batiisaama a seedantake yeeso yimɓe wonde hannde a won nder Allah e yimɓe makko, laaɓal ngal.

Yeesu noddinii almuɓɓe makko ngam yahde e waɗugo almuɓɓe nder leƴƴi fof, batiisatooɓe ɓe e innde Baaba, Ɓii e Ruuhu Potal (Matta 28:18–20). Naatugol nder laaɓal gure ko no a heɓortoo battanere, wa’ata kuugal, e juutna yahdu.

studyOnboardingSlides.body1: Once you have decided to be baptised, there are practical steps to work through with your church leader: fixing a date and place, deciding who to invite, choosing what to wear, and preparing what you will say — your testimony of what Jesus has done for you. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.
studyOnboardingSlides.body2: Da zarar ka yanke shawarar a yi maka baftisma, akwai matakai na aiki da za ka bi tare da shugaban cocinku: saita kwanan wata da wuri, yanke shawara game da wa za ka gayyata, zabar abin da za ka sa, da kuma shirya abin da za ka faɗa — shahadarku ta abin da Yesu ya yi dominku. Taɓa ⓘ kusa da kowane bayanin nassi don karanta nassin ba tare da barin manhajar ba.
studyOnboardingSlides.body3: Caggal ma yantirii batiisaade, gaa huɗɗe goɗɗe kuuɗe ngenndam hawrunɗe bee ganndo laaɓal ngal: siftorgo datal e nokku, humpitaago ko bomɓo noddataa, suɓugo ko waɗataa, e heɓtoraago ko a wownitaa — seede ma dow ɗume Yeesu waɗannoo ma. Mem ⓘ ɓadi kooɗume nassi ngam janngugo nassi on tawa a urtaayi nder manhaja nden.

sections.heading2: Don Me Aka Yi Wannan Nazarin?

blocks.title1: People who have recently become Christians
blocks.title2: Mutanen da kwanan nan suka zama Kiristoci
blocks.body1: If you have recently put your trust in Jesus Christ and want to understand what baptism is and whether it is for you, this study is written for you. It works through three Bible passages that explain who baptism is for, what it means, and how it connects you to God’s people.
blocks.body2: Idan kwanan nan ka dogara da Yesu Almasihu kuma kana son fahimtar abin da baftisma yake da kuma ko yana gare ka, wannan nazarin an rubuta shi dominku. Yana duba nassosi uku na Littafi Mai Tsarki da ke bayyana wa baftisma yake gare shi, abin da yake nufi, da kuma yadda yake haɗa ka da mutanen Allah.
blocks.body3: To a taccii Yeesu Almasiihu hannde nden a yiɗi anndugo ko batiisma woni e so waɗi ngam ma, janngirde nde ɗon winndaa ngam ma. Ɗum ɗon wolwa dow nassiije tati Linjiila ɗe fassirta ko bomɓo batiisma waɗi, ko ɗum hollitta, e no ɗum gasa ma bee yimɓe Allah.

blocks.title2: Mutanen da suke da tambayoyi game da baftisma
blocks.body1: You may have grown up in a church but never been baptised yourself, or you may have been baptised as a baby and are wondering whether that counts. This study does not assume any prior knowledge. It starts from the beginning and works through what the Bible actually says, with space to ask your own questions along the way.
blocks.body2: Watakila ka girma a coci amma ba a taɓa yi maka baftisma ba, ko kuma an yi maka baftisma a lokacin yarinta kuma kana mamaki ko hakan ya isa. Wannan nazarin baya ɗauka cewa kana da wani ilimi na baya. Yana fara daga farko kuma yana duba abin da Littafi Mai Tsarki ke faɗa hakika, tare da damar yin tambayoyinka a kan hanya.
blocks.body3: Waɗii a janngi nder laaɓal kono a batiisaaka hoore ma, koo batiisaama to piilugol e ɗon miijitii so ɗum moƴƴi. Janngirde nde tammaaki a mari anndal timmuɗam aran. Ɗum fuɗɗorta fuɗɗorde nden wolwa dow ko Linjiila wi’i tawaaɗum, bee nokkuure ngam ƴamugo ƴameeje ma e laawol.

blocks.body1: This study is suitable for young people who are considering baptism, provided a church leader and, where possible, your parents are involved. Faith is your own, and so is this decision — but it is not one to make alone. The book gives specific guidance on how to think through baptism with your parents if they have questions or concerns.
blocks.body2: Wannan nazarin ya dace da matasa da ke tunanin baftisma, muddin shugaban coci da, idan zai yiwu, iyayenka suna ciki. Imani naka ne, haka kuma wannan shawara — amma ba ita ce za a yanke ita kaɗai ba. Littafin yana ba da jagoranci na musamman kan yadda za ka yi tunani game da baftisma tare da iyayenka idan suna da tambayoyi ko damuwa.
blocks.body3: Janngirde nde moƴƴi ngam sukaaɓe miijiiɓe batiisma, so ganndo laaɓal e, haa ɗume woni, maaɓe on ngoni e dow. Tawagol woni ta ma, hono ɗum yantirgol on fuu — kono ɗum moƴƴaani yantugo tan hoore ma. Deftere nde hollitiima jagoramol feere dow no miijirtaa batiisma bee maaɓe to ɓe mari ƴameeje koo haaleele.

blocks.title1: Christians who want to understand baptism more deeply
blocks.title2: Kiristoci da ke son fahimtar baftisma sosai
blocks.body1: Even if you were baptised some time ago, working through this study can deepen your understanding of what your baptism meant and still means. The three Bible passages — Acts 2, Romans 6, and Matthew 28 — open up the full weight of what it means to be united with Christ, washed clean, and joined to his people.
blocks.body2: Ko da an yi maka baftisma lokaci da ya wuce, nazarin wannan zai iya ƙara zurfafa fahimtarka game da abin da baftismarka ya nufawa da kuma har yanzu yana nufi. Nassosi uku na Littafi Mai Tsarki — Ayyuka 2, Romawa 6, da Matiyu 28 — suna buɗe cikakken nauyin abin da yake nufi a haɗa da Almasihu, a wankewa, da kuma a shiga cikin mutanensa.
blocks.body3: Koo batiisaama ɗoon hawriri, waɗugo janngirde nde ɗon ɓeydugo anndugo ko batiisma ma hollitii e ko ɗum ɗon hollita hannde. Nassiije tati Linjiila ɗee — Kuuɗe 2, Romanko’en 6, e Matta 28 — ɗon yiltina teddeendi timmiindi ko fassirtaa laataago nguuɗo e Almasiihu, lotaado, e naataago nder yimɓe makko.

sections.heading2: Yadda Ake Amfani Da Wannan Nazarin

blocks.title1: Work through it with a church leader
blocks.title2: Yi shi tare da shugaban coci
blocks.body1: This study is designed to be worked through alongside a church leader. You can read each section on your own first and write your answers, then meet with your church leader to talk through what you found. Or you can read and discuss it together from the start. Either way, your church leader is there to help you understand, answer your questions, and guide you towards your baptism.
blocks.body2: An tsara wannan nazarin ne don a yi shi tare da shugaban coci. Kana iya karanta kowane ɓangare da kanka da farko ka rubuta amsoshinka, sannan ka haɗu da shugaban cocinku don tattauna abin da ka gano. Ko kuma kuna iya karanta shi tare da tattauna shi tun farko. Ko ta yaya, shugaban cocinku yana nan don taimaka maka fahimta, amsa tambayoyinka, da kuma jagorantarka zuwa ga baftismarka.
blocks.body3: Janngirde nde ɗon winndaa ngam waɗugo bee ganndo laaɓal. A waɗan janngugo kooɗume tekkudi hoore ma aran nden winnda jaabaawol ma, nden pota bee ganndo laaɓal ngal ngam tattaunago ko a keɓi. Koo on waɗan janngugo e tattaunago fof kawtal fuɗɗorde. Ko waawi, ganndo laaɓal ngal ɗon woni ngam wallitugo ma anndugo, jaabɗo ƴameeje ma, e ardaango ma to batiisma ma.

blocks.body1: Each of the first three sections has a Bible passage to read, questions about that passage to help you look carefully at the text, and space to write your answers. Section four is more practical — it gives you space to plan the details of your baptism. At the back of the study there is also a bonus section that answers common questions people ask.
blocks.body2: Kowane ɓangare daga cikin ɓangarori uku na farko yana da nassin Littafi Mai Tsarki don karanta, tambayoyi game da wannan nassin don taimaka maka duba rubutun da kyau, da kuma sarari don rubuta amsoshinka. Ɓangare na huɗu ya fi zama na aiki — yana ba ka sarari don tsara cikakkun bayanai na baftismarka. A bayan nazarin akwai kuma sashen ƙarin bayani wanda ke amsa tambayoyin da mutane sukan yi.
blocks.body3: Kooɗume tekkudi nder tati araneeje ɗon mari nassi Linjiila ngam janngugo, ƴameeje dow nassi ngon ngam wallitugo ma ndara ruunde e ruunde, e nokkuure ngam winndude jaabaawol ma. Tekkudi nayaɓo on ɓuri laataago ko huutortee — ɗum ɗon hokka ma nokkuure ngam daftaago kuɗɗe batiisma ma. Haa ragare janngirde ɗon woni fuu tekkudi ɓeydingol nde jaabata ƴameeje ɗe yimɓe ɓuri ƴamugo.

blocks.title1: Take your time — but do not put it off
blocks.title2: Yi hakuri — amma kada ka jinkirta
blocks.title3: Hokku hoore ma wakkati — kono taa a ɓoornu
blocks.body1: There is no rush to work through this study, and it is fine to sit with a question that is hard. At the same time, the New Testament consistently shows that people were baptised soon after coming to faith — not because they had all the answers, but because baptism is a command of Jesus and his people want to obey him. The aim of this study is not to delay your baptism but to help you move towards it with confidence.
blocks.body2: Babu gaggawa wajen nazarin wannan, kuma lafiya ne ka zauna da tambayar da ke da wuya. A lokaci guda, Sabon Alkawari yana nuna akai-akai cewa mutane sun yi baftisma ba da dadewa ba bayan sun zo imani — ba don suna da dukkan amsoshi ba, amma saboda baftisma umurnin Yesu ne kuma mutanensa suna son biyayya gare shi. Manufar wannan nazarin ba ita ce jinkirta baftismarka ba amma taimaka maka kusantar da shi da kwarin gwiwa.
blocks.body3: Walaa herwude ngam waɗugo janngirde nde, e moƴƴi siwtude dow ƴamde caɗtunde. Kono e wakkati gooto, Ahdu Pamɗo ɗon hollita wakkati fuu yimɓe batiisaama caggal taccagol law — naa ko ɓe njiimi jaabaaji fof, amma ko batiisma ko Yeesu yamiri nden yimɓe makko ɓon yiɗi jafaago. Ɗabbunde janngirde nde naa ngam ɓoornaago batiisma ma amma ngam wallitugo ma yahde to maajum bee temmingol.

blocks.title1: A guide, not just an examiner
blocks.title2: Mai jagoranci, ba mai jarrabawa kawai ba
blocks.body1:
Your church leader is not there to test whether you have the right answers. They are there to walk alongside you — to explain things that are unclear, to help you think through your own faith, to answer the questions in the bonus section that you want to discuss, and to help you plan and prepare for your baptism.

Use the Share button at the bottom of each section to send your written answers to your church leader before you meet. This gives them a window into your thinking and makes for a much richer conversation.
blocks.body2:
Shugaban cocinku ba yana nan don jarrabawa ko kana da amsoshi daidai ba. Yana nan don tafiya tare da kai — don bayyana abubuwan da ba su bayyana ba, don taimaka maka yin tunani game da imaninka, don amsa tambayoyin da ke sashen ƙarin bayani da kake son tattaunawa, da kuma don taimaka maka tsarawa da shirya baftismarka.

Yi amfani da maballin Raba a ƙasan kowane ɓangare don aika amsoshinka zuwa ga shugaban cocinku kafin ku haɗu. Wannan yana ba su damar ganin tunaninka kuma yana sa tattaunawar ta kasance mai fa’ida sosai.
blocks.body3:
Ganndo laaɓal ngal ɗon wonataa ngam jarabaago so a mari jaabaaji goonga. Ɗon woni ngam yahdugo tawa ma — ngam fassirago huunde moƴƴaani bayinaama, ngam wallitugo ma miijitago tawagol ma, ngam jaabago ƴameeje nder tekkudi ɓeydingol ngo a yiɗi tattaunago, e ngam wallitugo ma daftaago e heɓtoraago batiisma ma.

Naftir buton Yeeyu haa les kooɗume tekkudi ngam neldugo jaabaawol ma winndungol to ganndo laaɓal bako on pota. Ɗum ɗon hokka ɓe laawol yi’ugo numtol ma nden waɗan tattaunawa ndun mawnugo sanne.

blocks.title1: If you are a young person
blocks.body1: If you are a young person thinking about baptism, it is important that your parents are involved in the process. This may not feel easy, but your church leader will know how to help your parents understand what baptism is about and why you want to be baptised. The bonus section at the back of the study also addresses this question directly.
blocks.body2: Idan kai matashi ne da ke tunanin baftisma, yana da muhimmanci cewa iyayenka suna cikin wannan tsari. Wannan na iya zama ba sauƙi ba, amma shugaban cocinku zai san yadda ake taimaka wa iyayenka fahimtar abin da baftisma yake nufi da kuma dalilin da ya sa kake son yin baftisma. Sashen ƙarin bayani a bayan nazarin kuma yana magana akan wannan tambayar kai tsaye.
blocks.body3: To a sukaaɗo miijiiɗo batiisma, ɗum haani maaɓe on ngona e dow laawol ngon. Ɗum waɗaali ɓuri moƴƴude wakkati fuu, kono ganndo laaɓal ngal anndi no wallirta maaɓe anndugo ko batiisma hollitta e ko waɗi a yiɗi batiisaade. Tekkudi ɓeydingol ngol ragare janngirde ɗon fuu wolwa dow ƴamde nde ɗon timmuki.

blocks.body1: Each of the first three sections has two kinds of questions. The passage questions ask you to look carefully at the Bible text — what it actually says, what words mean, and what is happening in the story. The personal questions ask how the passage connects to your own life and faith — where you are now, and what you need to think through.
blocks.body2: Kowane ɓangare daga cikin ɓangarori uku na farko yana da ire-iren tambayoyi biyu. Tambayoyin nassi suna neman ka duba rubutun Littafi Mai Tsarki da kyau — abin da yake faɗa hakika, abin da kalmomi ke nufi, da abin da ke faruwa a labarin. Tambayoyin sirri suna tambayar yadda nassin ke da alaƙa da rayuwarka da imaninka — inda kake yanzu, da kuma abin da kake buƙatar tunani game da shi.
blocks.body3: Kooɗume tekkudi nder tati araneeje ɗon mari irin ƴameeje ɗiɗi. Ƴameeje nassi ɗon ƴama ma ndara ruunde e ruunde dow ruunde Linjiila — ko ɗum wi’i tawaaɗum, ko kelmeeji hollitta, e ko waɗata e tindol ngol. Ƴameeje hoore neɗɗo ɗon ƴama no nassi on gasa bee ngendam ma e tawagol ma — hol to a woni hannde, e ko a haani miijitago.

blocks.title1: You do not need to have all the answers
blocks.title2: Ba lazimi ka sami dukkan amsoshi ba
blocks.body1: The questions are not a test. If you are not sure about something, say so — that is exactly what your church leader is there to help with. The bonus section at the back addresses many of the most common questions people have, including questions about infant baptism, sprinkling versus immersion, whether you need to be baptised to take communion, and what to do if your parents object. If your question is not there, ask your church leader.
blocks.body2: Tambayoyin ba jarabawa ba ne. Idan ba ka da tabbas game da wani abu, faɗa haka — wannan shi ne ainihin abin da shugaban cocinku yake nan don taimakawa. Sashen ƙarin bayani a bayan yana magana game da yawancin tambayoyin da mutane sukan yi, ciki har da tambayoyi game da baftismar jarirai, fesawa da kuma nutsuwa, ko kana buƙatar baftisma don shan Sakaramanti, da kuma abin da za ka yi idan iyayenka sun ƙi. Idan tambayarka ba ta can ba, tambaya shugaban cocinku.
blocks.body3: Ƴameeje ɗee naa jarabaaki on. To a moƴƴaani anndugo dow huunde, wi’u ɗum — ko ɗum timmuki waɗi ganndo laaɓal ngal ngam wallitugo. Tekkudi ɓeydingol ngol ragare ɗon wolwa dow heewi ƴameeje ɗe yimɓe ɓuri ƴamugo, ɗum heewi ƴameeje dow batiisma piilugol, faccude koo njiimuɗam, so haaniima batiisaade ngam naatude nder njaamri, e ko waɗata to maaɓe ngalaa yiɗude. To ƴamde ma walaa ɗoon, ƴam ganndo laaɓal ngal.

intro1: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each chapter – both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have worked through the questions personally before they help others doing the same.

Create a safe space for honesty. The questions in this study are designed to prompt real reflection, not just correct answers. Your job is to make the group feel safe enough to say what they actually think, not what they think a Christian is supposed to say.
Don’t rush the silences. Some of the best moments in a group study happen after a question lands and nobody speaks for a few seconds. Resist the urge to fill every silence.
Watch for the person who goes quiet. Sometimes the most significant thing happening in the room is with the person who says the least. Check in with them afterwards.
intro2: Wadannan bayanan an tsara su ne don taimaka wa shugabannin rukuni da masu jagoranci su sami fa’ida sosai daga kowane babi – ga mutanen da suke jagora da kuma kansu. Ba madadin nazarin kayan aikin da kanka ba ne da farko. Mafi kyawun shugabanni su ne waɗanda suka yi nazarin tambayoyin da kansu kafin su taimaka wa wasu yin hakan.

Ka samar da yanayi mai aminci don gaskiya. Tambayoyin da ke cikin wannan nazarin an tsara su ne don motsa tunani na kwarai, ba kawai amsoshi daidai ba. Aikinka shi ne ka sa rukunin su ji suna cikin aminci da za su faɗi abin da suke tunani na gaske, ba abin da suke tsammanin ya kamata Kirista ya faɗa ba.

Kada ka hanzarta lokacin shiru. Wasu daga cikin mafi kyawun lokuta a nazarin rukuni suna faruwa bayan an gabatar da tambaya kuma babu wanda ya yi magana na ƴan daƙiƙoƙi. Ka guji sha’awar cike kowane shiru.
Ka kula da mutumin da ya yi shiru. Wani lokaci abu mafi mahimmanci da ke faruwa a dakin shi ne tare da mutumin da ya fi kowa ƙarancin magana. Ka duba su bayan an gama.
intro3: Ɗee kuuje binndaɗe njiɗi nafaago ardiiɓe e ekkitinooɓe heɓugo nafa haa kooɗume babi – ngam yimɓe ɓe ɓe ardoto e ngam ko’e maɓɓe kadi. Ɗum wonaa mbadal waɗugo kuugal ngal a anndi kanko on aran. Ardiiɓe ɓuri mawnugo ngoni ɓeen waɗuɓe ƴameeje ɗee dow ko’e maɓɓe bako ɓe mballa woɓɓe waɗugo bana non.

Tiggan nokkuure hoolare ngam goonga. Ƴameeje nder janngirde nde njiɗi ummugo miijitago goonga, naa tan jaabaawol goongawol. Kuugal ma on waɗugo yimɓe nder kawtal ngal nannira hoore maɓɓe nder hoolare haa ɓe wolwa ko ɓe numata goonga, naa ko ɓe numata nassi mo Kirista’en poti wolwugo.
Taa henyu de’irre. Wakkatiiji ɓurɗi mawnugo nder janngirde kawtal ɗon laata ɓaawo ƴameende fukkaama de’irre laati ɗon haa seɗɗa. Taa nannu mbeebake cukkugo kooɗume de’irre.
Ndarna gite ma dow neɗɗo de’uɗo. Wakkati feere, huunde ɓurnde mawnugo ɗon laata nder suudu ndun woni bee neɗɗo pamaro wolde on. Ndara ɓe ɓaawo ɗon.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This opening chapter establishes the framework for the whole study: the Christian life begins and continues with repentance and faith. Neither can be left behind.

The ‘Growth Graph’ concept is central. Use it to get the group to locate themselves honestly — are they on a Graph 3 trajectory (uneven but growing) or a Graph 4 (stalled)? This is not a trick question; the honest answer is the useful one.

Draw out the link between sin and darkness (1 John 1:5-7): the things we keep hidden tend to grow. Repentance is not just a one-off act at conversion — it is the ongoing posture of the Christian life.
chapters.pastorals1:
Some people in the group may feel stuck and ashamed about it. The chapter’s tone is not condemnatory — it is hopeful. Graph 4 can become Graph 5. Make sure that message lands clearly.

Others may be tempted to tick ‘Graph 3’ automatically without real reflection. Encourage honest self-assessment; the growth graph exercise only works if people engage with it genuinely.

Watch for people who are embarrassed about ongoing sin (1 John 1:8-9). The chapter addresses this directly: pretending we have no struggles breeds hypocrisy and stalls growth. Help the group see that acknowledging sin to God — and to safe others — is the path forward, not a sign of failure.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who equate spiritual maturity with having fewer struggles. The chapter argues the opposite: mature Christians are more honest about their struggles, not less.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter explores what it means to grow in Jesus: in obedience, in grace, and in knowledge of Christ. These three are not alternatives — they belong together.

The septic tank illustration is deliberately provocative. Use it to press the question: do we actually want to be saved from sin, or just from its consequences? Salvation and discipleship must go together (Matthew 28:19-20).

The section on grace and godliness (Titus 2:12; Romans 5:6-8, 10) is theologically important. Christ didn’t save us because we learned to obey Him. We obey because He has already saved us. This ordering matters enormously for how people approach the Christian life.
chapters.pastorals1:
Paul’s story at the start will resonate with many in the group — the cooling-off pattern is very common. Normalise it, but don’t leave it there: the point is that it doesn’t have to stay that way.

Some may struggle with the idea that God’s love doesn’t depend on their performance. This is deeply counter-intuitive for many people. Take time with Romans 5:6-8 — Christ died for us while we were still sinners, not once we had sorted ourselves out.

The Heart Check Likert scale at the end is designed to surface honest self-awareness. Encourage people to answer it privately and truthfully before any group discussion.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who hear ‘grace’ and conclude that obedience doesn’t matter. The chapter is clear that grace produces godliness (Titus 2:12), not a licence to coast.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter presses the distinction between ageing and maturing. Time passes automatically; growth does not. Some people have been Christians for decades and are still stuck at an early stage of maturity — and that is not what God intends.

The passages from Colossians, Psalm 1, and Mark 4 together build a picture of what roots look like in practice: staying rooted in Christ (Col 2:6-7), meditating on God’s Word (Ps 1:2-3), and not allowing the thorns of life to choke growth (Mark 4:7).

The Gospel-Growth Plan section is the practical centrepiece of the chapter. It asks people to identify one specific, personal growth area — not a vague aspiration but a concrete target. This is worth significant discussion time.
chapters.pastorals1:
People may find it uncomfortable to be asked to name a specific growth area. Some will default to something safe and general. Gently push for something real — the exercise only has value if it’s honest.

Be sensitive to the fact that some growth areas (addiction, secret sin, relationship breakdown) may be deeply painful to acknowledge, even privately. The app format gives people privacy; use that as a feature, not a limitation.

The amazing destiny passage (Philippians 3:20-21) is meant to be genuinely motivating. Don’t rush past it — growth is not mere duty; it is moving towards something glorious.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who choose a Gospel-growth area that is really about someone else (‘my marriage’, ‘my family’). The plan should be personal and individual.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter introduces sin as a predator. John Owen’s quote — ‘Be killing sin or it will be killing you’ — frames everything that follows. Sin is not a minor inconvenience; it is actively destructive.

The study of Genesis 3 repays careful attention. The serpent’s tactics — questioning God’s word, distorting His commands, promising what He hasn’t promised — are still in use today. Help the group see how these tactics work in their own experience of temptation.

The section on sin spreading (Genesis 4) shows how quickly sin escalates: from one act of disobedience to fratricide within a generation. The weed-seed illustration makes the point well — small sins are not harmless; they grow.
chapters.pastorals1:
Thomas’s story at the start is deliberately confronting. Some in the group may have their own version of it. The aim is not to shock, but to be honest that sin’s effects can be devastating — and that there is a way back.

The question ‘In your life, who is doing better at killing who?’ is intentionally direct. Give people space to sit with that question before moving on. Silence here is often productive.

Watch for people who minimise their sin by comparing it to others’. The half-truth dynamic (Genesis 3) is worth pressing: the most dangerous lies have some truth mixed in.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who have carried hidden sin for a long time and may feel that their situation is beyond hope. The hints of grace in Genesis 3:15 and 3:21 are there for a reason — God has not given up on His purposes of blessing.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter explores sin as refusal — refusal of God’s knowledge, His authority, His goodness, and ultimately of God Himself. Reinhold Niebuhr’s opening quote frames sin as the refusal to accept our creaturely limits.

The sequence of passages builds a cumulative picture of what sin refuses: knowledge (Romans 1:18-23), God’s authority (Romans 5:14; Ezekiel 2:3-5), gratitude (Romans 1:21b), relationship (Leviticus 26:40), and finally God Himself (Romans 1:25).

The contrast with Mark 12:30 is important: the opposite of refusing God is loving Him with everything. Use this to move the discussion from diagnosis to aspiration.

The closing bridge text on sin hierarchies is worth dwelling on — Christians often tolerate invisible sins (pride, greed, envy) while policing visible ones. Jesus didn’t operate that way.
chapters.pastorals1:
This chapter is theologically dense. Don’t feel you need to cover every passage in a group setting — pick the two or three that are most relevant to where your group is.

The question ‘Which of these types of refusals are you most at risk of?’ is very personal. It may produce either very honest answers or very guarded ones. Either response is revealing.

The Pharisee dynamic in the closing bridge (Mark 2:15-17) is important pastorally: some group members may be very good at external religion while harbouring deep interior refusals. Handle with care.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who identify easily with the ‘refusal of authority’ examples but are blind to their own refusal of gratitude or relationship with God. Both are forms of the same thing.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter turns from the diagnosis of sin to the Father’s love. The parable of the lost son (Luke 15:11-32) is the primary text, explored through the lens of its three main characters.

The younger son represents the person who has walked away from God openly. His journey — from demanding his inheritance to feeding pigs to ‘coming to his senses’ — maps onto many people’s experience of sin and its consequences.

The father’s response is the theological heart of the chapter: he sees his son ‘while he was still a long way off’, runs to him, and restores him fully. This is not a grudging acceptance — it is extravagant welcome. The robe, the ring, the fattened calf: full restoration, not probation.

The bipolar Likert scale at the start — measuring how the group perceives God’s attitude towards them — sets up a before/after comparison with chapter 7. It’s worth completing carefully.
chapters.pastorals1:
Some in the group will identify painfully with the younger son. Be ready for that. The chapter’s message is one of hope: the father hasn’t given up, and neither has God.

The distinction between leaving through the ‘front door’ and leaving through the ‘back door’ is pastorally significant. Some people in church settings have never actually come home to God — they’ve just stayed in the vicinity. Handle this gently but honestly.

The question ‘Do you mostly feel at home with God, or more distant?’ is a key pastoral barometer. Don’t rush past it.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who have a deeply negative view of God — as distant, judgmental, or indifferent. The parable is specifically designed to correct that picture. Let it do its work.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter completes the parable by focusing on the older son and the listening audience. Jesus originally told the parable to the Pharisees — people who were religious but distant from God in a different way.

The older son’s problem is not irreligion but a transactional view of his relationship with the father. He thinks he has earned love through obedience. He is miles from his father emotionally even while living in his house. This is a searching challenge for churchgoing people.

The ‘tower block’ illustration challenges the common assumption that salvation is about being good enough. Titus 3:5 and Galatians 2:16 make clear that no one is justified by works — God cuts the building vertically, not horizontally.

The repeated bipolar Likert scale at the end allows people to compare their perception of God’s attitude towards them before and after engaging with the parable. This can be a powerful moment of reflection.
chapters.pastorals1:
The older son dynamic is often harder to surface in a group than the younger son dynamic. People who identify with the older son are often high-functioning, respectable, and resistant to the idea that they might be ‘outside the party’.

The questions ‘Am I more like the younger son or the older one?’ and ‘Am I inside the party or outside?’ are the most important in the chapter. Give them real time and real space.

The closing comparison of the two Likert scales (from chapters 6 and 7) is an opportunity for a genuinely moving moment — if people have engaged honestly, they may find that the parable has shifted something in how they see God. Be attentive to that.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who give intellectual assent to the gospel (‘yes, I know God loves me’) without any emotional reality to it. The parable is designed to move people, not just inform them.

paras: Jay Parsons is the Associate Minister at Chalmers Church, Edinburgh, UK

Jay oversees work with children and young people, membership and Global Missions at Chalmers.  He has a significant role in pastoral care and is part of the preaching team.  After studying at Oak Hill Jay worked for 10 years on the staff team at Christ Church Banstead before moving to Edinburgh in 2020.
Jay is married to Jo and they have three children. Jay is still holding onto the idea that he will one day play centre forward for Leeds United.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).
